skill.name: log-analyzer
skill.description: Analyze application logs to find errors, trends, anomalies, performance issues, and practical incident recommendations.
skill.summary: Turn raw logs into structured findings, timelines, metrics, and troubleshooting guidance.
skill.author: CuriousLearner

security_audit.summary: Static analysis reported many high-risk patterns, but review found they are Markdown fences, sample logs, private IP examples, and basic grep or awk examples. No evidence found for malicious intent, prompt injection, credential exfiltration, weak cryptographic code, network access, or command scheduling. The remaining risk is low and relates to handling sensitive log files and optional local shell examples.

low_findings.title: Local Shell Examples for Log Filtering
low_findings.description: The skill includes grep, awk, cut, sort, and uniq examples for local log analysis. These examples are not automatically executed, but users should run them only against intended log files and review paths before execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The referenced lines contain concrete shell commands, but they are examples inside Markdown. They read local log files and do not show network transfer, destructive actions, or command construction from untrusted input.

low_findings.title: Sensitive Log Data Exposure Risk
low_findings.description: The skill is designed to inspect logs that may contain user identifiers, IP addresses, endpoints, stack traces, and internal service details. This is expected for a log analyzer, but users should redact secrets and personal data before sharing output.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The skill explicitly analyzes user IDs, failed authentication attempts, IP addresses, and request activity. This is legitimate functionality, but it creates a clear privacy and data-handling consideration.

low_findings.title: Static Scanner Findings Are Mostly False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported Ruby backtick, weak cryptography, hardcoded IP, and at-scheduler detections align with Markdown code fences, sample values, private IP examples, and ordinary prose. No evidence found of actual Ruby execution, cryptographic implementation, network calls, or scheduled command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review of the cited lines shows examples and prose rather than executable dangerous behavior. The high confidence comes from direct line inspection across the flagged categories.

content.value_statement: Large log files make incidents hard to understand quickly. This skill structures logs into errors, timelines, metrics, patterns, and practical next steps.
actual_capabilities: Identifies common log formats such as JSON, syslog-style, Apache-style, and custom application logs.
Extracts timestamps, severity levels, messages, services, user IDs, endpoints, and stack trace context.
Summarizes error frequency, repeated messages, spike windows, and service-level impact.
Builds incident timelines that connect traffic changes, resource pressure, and error rates.
Reports performance metrics such as average latency, percentiles, slow endpoints, and request volume.
Recommends fixes, monitoring alerts, and logging improvements based on observed patterns.
limitations: It does not automatically collect logs from external systems.
It depends on the quality and completeness of the provided log data.
It may need user context to distinguish normal behavior from incidents.
It should not receive unredacted secrets, credentials, or regulated personal data.

use_cases.title: Investigate Production Incidents
use_cases.description: Analyze recent application logs to find the first error, peak impact window, likely root cause, and immediate remediation steps.

use_cases.title: Improve Application Monitoring
use_cases.description: Review historical logs to identify missing fields, useful alerts, noisy events, and better structured logging practices.

use_cases.title: Debug Customer-Reported Failures
use_cases.description: Trace errors around a user ID, request ID, endpoint, or time window and summarize what failed.

prompt_templates.title: Summarize Log Health
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner review of a small log excerpt.
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these application logs. Summarize the main errors, warning patterns, affected services, and likely impact.

prompt_templates.title: Find Incident Timeline
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this log file for the time range I provide. Build a timeline of the incident, identify the first failure, and list likely root causes.

prompt_templates.title: Measure Performance Patterns
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these access logs for response time patterns. Report average, p95, p99, slow endpoints, peak traffic periods, and anomalies.

prompt_templates.title: Design Monitoring Improvements
prompt_templates.prompt: Analyze these logs and propose alert rules, structured logging changes, dashboards, and retention recommendations for this service.

output_examples.input: A one-hour application log excerpt with repeated database connection errors.
output_examples.output: The report identifies the first connection failure, the peak error window, affected services, probable pool exhaustion, and recommended pool and timeout changes.

output_examples.input: Access logs from an API with response times and status codes.
output: A latency summary with average, p95, and p99 values.
A ranked list of slow endpoints and error-heavy routes.
Recommendations for caching, pagination, and alert thresholds.

output_examples.input: Authentication logs containing failed login attempts over one day.
output_examples.output: The analysis separates normal failures from suspicious bursts, highlights affected accounts, and recommends rate limits and alert rules.

best_practices: Redact secrets, tokens, session IDs, and regulated personal data before sharing logs.
Provide the time zone, incident window, service names, and deployment context with each analysis request.
Ask for both findings and evidence so each recommendation links back to observed log patterns.
anti_patterns: Do not paste complete production logs when a narrowed time window is enough.
Do not treat correlation in logs as confirmed causation without system context.
Do not run suggested shell commands against broad directories or sensitive paths.

faq.question: What log formats can this skill analyze?
faq.answer: It can work with JSON logs, common web server logs, syslog-style entries, and custom application logs.

faq.question: Can it find the root cause of an incident?
faq.answer: It can propose likely root causes from log evidence, but final confirmation may need metrics, traces, deployments, and system context.

faq.question: Does it connect to logging platforms?
faq.answer: No. The skill discusses tools such as Kibana, Splunk, Datadog, CloudWatch, and Loki, but it does not connect to them.

faq.question: Is it safe to use with production logs?
faq.answer: Use it with redacted and scoped logs. Remove credentials, tokens, session identifiers, and sensitive personal data first.

faq.question: Can it analyze performance problems?
faq.answer: Yes. It can summarize response times, slow endpoints, traffic peaks, and performance anomalies when logs contain timing fields.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use this skill?
faq.answer: Yes. The report lists compatibility with Claude, Codex, and Claude Code.
